title_en: The Creed of the Early Muslims: Its Sources and Its Authority
content_en:
Al-Aqeedah al-Salafiyyah opens with an account of what is meant by 'the creed of the Salaf' — the belief system of the early generations of Muslims who received the Quran and Sunnah directly or in the closest proximity to their revelation. The term al-salaf al-salih (the righteous early generations) refers primarily to the Companions of the Prophet (as-sahabah), their successors (at-tabi'un), and the successors of the successors (atba' at-tabi'in) — a period spanning roughly the first three centuries of the Islamic calendar. The Prophet, peace be upon him, referred to these generations directly in the well-known hadith: 'The best of people are my generation, then those who follow them, then those who follow them.'

The authority of Salafi creed rests on a foundational conviction: those who lived closest to the time of revelation, who received it from the Prophet or from those who received it from him, and who applied it in the most direct religious environment, are the most reliable interpreters of what the Quran and Sunnah actually teach. This does not mean that later scholarship is irrelevant — the great imams of fiqh and aqeedah built on the Salafi tradition. But it means that any later theological position that contradicts the documented understanding of the Companions and the early scholars is presumed to be in error.

The work surveys the primary sources from which Salafi creed is reconstructed: first, the Quran itself in its apparent meaning; second, the authentic hadith of the Prophet; third, the reported statements (athar) of the Companions on theological matters; fourth, the statements of the major tabi'in scholars such as Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, al-Hasan al-Basri, Ibrahim an-Nakha'i, and Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz; and fifth, the creeds of the early imams — including those documented for Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, Imam al-Bukhari, and Imam at-Tahawi — which codified the Salafi understanding for future generations.

The work then addresses a common objection: that the Salaf did not have a systematic theology and therefore the concept of 'Salafi aqeedah' is a retroactive construction. The response is that while the Salaf did not write systematic philosophical treatises — they were not philosophers — they consistently affirmed and repeatedly stated specific theological positions in response to the early heresies that arose. When the Jahmiyyah denied divine attributes, the scholars of the Salaf responded clearly. When the Mu'tazilah denied divine decree, the Salafi scholars responded. When the Khawarij made major sin equivalent to kufr, the scholars of the Salaf responded. This pattern of response constitutes a coherent and consistent theological tradition, even if it was not organized into a philosophical system.

The introductory chapter concludes by distinguishing Salafi creed from mere traditionalism or conservatism. The Salaf were not simply people who lived long ago whose views are followed out of historical nostalgia. They are followed because they were the best interpreters of divine revelation, the closest to the prophetic light, and the most free from the later influences — Greek philosophy, Persian mysticism, Indian religious thought — that introduced distortions into parts of the Islamic theological tradition. Following their creed is, in this understanding, following the most authentic available version of the prophetic teaching.
